gist: A multi-guest 0xResearch farewell roundtable diagnosing a sharp crypto drawdown: a 60% Zcash crash, $1.4B of June BTC ETF outflows, MicroStrategy/Saylor's three-asset (equity/BTC/STRC-stretch) reflexive leverage trap, drying DeFi lending yields, hyperliquid (HYPE) treasury flow dynamics, and a skeptical verdict on token buyback-and-burn as a value driver. Conviction picks cluster around HYPE, Maple (Syrup), MetaDAO, and revenue/transparency-backed tokens.

insights.claim: BTC spot ETFs saw roughly $1.4B in outflows in just the first four to five days of June 2026, already tracking as the third- or fourth-worst month ever and on pace to potentially become the worst.
insights.speaker: Carlos (Blockworks Research)
insights.so_what: Persistent ETF redemptions signal institutional de-risking and a structural buyer turning into a seller; watch monthly flow prints as a near-term BTC floor indicator.
insights.quote: so far in June which is like four days five days it's been like 1.4 billion dollars in outflows which is already like the third or fourth fourth like worst month ever for the BTC ETFs

insights.claim: The market is in a 'good news is bad news' macro regime where a bullish jobs report is read bearishly because strong data reduces odds of Fed rate cuts, driving correlated selloffs across equities and crypto.
insights.speaker: Danny (Blockworks)
insights.so_what: In this regime, weak macro data is the catalyst risk assets need; position for crypto rallies on soft prints, not strong ones.
insights.quote: the bullish news is actually bearish news because people want bad data because they don't want the Fed to hike rates because the data is too good

insights.claim: A wave of high-profile IPOs and pre-IPOs (including a quantum-computing IPO and a SpaceX pre-IPO) is siphoning liquidity away from crypto and other risk assets.
insights.speaker: Toma (Blockworks Advisory)
insights.so_what: Crypto faces a liquidity-competition headwind from a hot primary-issuance market; IPO outcomes (windfall vs catastrophe) could redirect flows back into or further away from crypto.
insights.quote: he thinks that the IPO's are siphoning liquidity off of other risk assets

insights.claim: Crypto exhibits an asymmetric correlation to equities where it falls on equity down-days but fails to rally on equity up-days, leaving it structurally disadvantaged in choppy markets.
insights.speaker: Jim
insights.so_what: This 'heads you lose, tails you don't win' dynamic argues against holding crypto beta versus equity beta in a volatile tape.
insights.quote: some crypto goes down when the markets go up and crypto goes down when the markets go down

insights.claim: Despite a bad feeling about the market, the risk-reward of shorting or selling BTC at current levels is unattractive, with the speaker leaning that price is closer to a bottom than not.
insights.speaker: Carlos (Blockworks Research)
insights.so_what: Several desk participants converge on 'closer to bottom' for BTC even amid bearish flows, implying limited downside conviction for fresh shorts.
insights.quote: I probably say we're more close to the bottom than not

insights.claim: Even if BTC is near a bottom, the opportunity cost of spending six months bottom-fishing crypto is high relative to outsized bull markets in Korean equities and 'memory stocks.'
insights.speaker: host (Boccaccio)
insights.so_what: Capital-allocation framing: crypto's risk-adjusted appeal is being eroded by hotter trades elsewhere, a reason flows leave even if BTC is cheap.
insights.quote: do you really want to like spend 6 months trying to pick the bottom at BTC when there's the greatest bull market ever known to mankind in Korea and memory stocks

insights.claim: MicroStrategy/Saylor has engineered his own 'trilemma' across three positions (the MSTR equity, the Bitcoin he wants to buy, and preferred vehicles like STRC/stretch), where supporting one asset typically requires selling another, creating a permanent reflexive loop.
insights.speaker: host (Boccaccio)
insights.so_what: The structure is self-cannibalizing: buyers of any single leg are 'exit liquidity' for another, a structural reason to avoid all three vehicles in a downturn.
insights.quote: he has to sell one of them to make the other one go up and then he has to sell that one to make that one go up

insights.claim: Saylor's reported sale of just $2 million worth of an asset (despite not needing to) functioned as a liquidity test and spooked the market because he is the largest holder, breaking the consensus belief that he would never sell.
insights.speaker: host (Boccaccio)
insights.so_what: The 'Saylor never sells' narrative is broken; any future sale headline is now a market-moving fear catalyst, raising tail risk for MSTR-linked assets.
insights.quote: the market expected him always to not sell. And he has to sell now. And he probably will still have to sell again

insights.claim: The STRC ('stretch') product is depegging (trading around $91) and requires Saylor to keep raising its yield to hold the peg, which forces him to sell either MSTR equity or Bitcoin to fund that yield.
insights.speaker: host (Boccaccio)
insights.so_what: STRC's peg is a live stress gauge for the whole Saylor complex; a falling STRC price forces selling that pressures BTC, a self-reinforcing downside spiral.

insights.claim: On-chain holders of STRC and APX-USD will feel pressure first in any Saylor unwind because the on-chain components react fastest, ahead of the equity or BTC legs.
insights.speaker: Matt (CMT Digital)
insights.so_what: Front-runs the 'waterfall' of pain: on-chain Saylor-derivative exposure is the first and most fragile leg to monitor and avoid.
insights.quote: the first people to feel heat will be the STRC holders APXUSD holders. It's just natural too cuz of the on-chain component

insights.claim: The only clean exit for Saylor that fully resolves the leverage is Bitcoin ripping to roughly $120K-$128K, and current reserve math gives him about one year of runway at present STRC yield rates.
insights.speaker: Aryan (CMT Digital)
insights.so_what: Sets a concrete bull-case price target and a ~12-month timeline; the probability of BTC reaching ~$120K within a year (judged 'low / coin-flip no') becomes the key bet for the entire complex.
insights.quote: the only clean exit that's fully available to him isn't really in his control and that's just Bitcoin ripping to 128 plus

insights.claim: An ~$40M position against tokenized STRK sits in Morpho with a still-healthy 1.09 health factor at ~85% LTV, while Andrew Hong/Herd data shows 50+ tokens intertwined within APX-USD exposure across Pendle, Morpho and other venues.
insights.speaker: Ian
insights.so_what: APX-USD is deeply integrated DeFi collateral; a sub-50% discount could trigger cascading liquidations across Pendle/Morpho, a contagion vector worth monitoring.
insights.quote: there's like 50 plus tokens involved that are intertwined within APX USD exposure across Pendle, Morpho

insights.claim: A confirmed Saylor sale announced on Monday could be bullish for depegged products because it would let assets like STRC re-peg quickly and relieve market fear.
insights.speaker: Jim
insights.so_what: Counterintuitive trade: a feared 'Saylor sells' headline could be the capitulation/relief catalyst that re-pegs distressed PTs (Saturn, Apex Finance) trading at deep discounts.
insights.quote: if we get an announcement on on Monday that sailor sold, I think that's probably pretty bullish cuz then like it'll it should re-peg pretty quickly

insights.claim: Saylor's preferred-issuance leverage is a reflexive flywheel that becomes harder to defend as BTC falls below his cost basis (estimated in the low-to-mid $60Ks), with the worst pain landing on STRC/APX-USD holders.
insights.speaker: Matt (CMT Digital)
insights.so_what: Below-cost-basis BTC is the key trigger level that flips the flywheel from virtuous to vicious; size positions to a max-pain scenario.

insights.claim: On-chain stablecoin lending yields have collapsed to roughly 1.5%-2.5% annualized as prices fell, with most assets trading below their October lows, making the smart-contract risk unattractive versus off-chain T-bills or high-yield savings.
insights.speaker: Matt (CMT Digital)
insights.so_what: Capital rationally rotates from on-chain yield to risk-free off-chain alternatives, draining DeFi TVL and pressuring lending tokens even though they have product-market fit.
insights.quote: you have call it 1 and 1/2 to 2 and 1/2% annualized yield on stablecoin rates generally across the board

insights.claim: DeFi lending is unwinding reflexively because participants became forced loopers (borrowing stables against stables) and protocols suppressed borrow rates to keep loops profitable; events like RS-ETH spiking borrow rates on Aave illustrate the recursive de-risking.
insights.speaker: Matt (CMT Digital)
insights.so_what: The same reflexivity that drove TVL up is now driving it down; looped lending strategies are the fragile core of the unwind to avoid or fade.
insights.quote: it's all this recursive unwind that's happening

insights.claim: Institutional DeFi adoption is gated by underwriting risk, and the solution is mandatory formal verification of smart-contract methods plus insurance/downside-protection providers underwriting strategies, mirroring TradFi norms.
insights.speaker: Matt (CMT Digital)
insights.so_what: Identifies smart-contract insurance and formal verification as an under-built market opportunity needed to unlock institutional capital into DeFi.
insights.quote: formal verification of smart contract methods should be a standard in my opinion

insights.claim: On-chain lending is bullish in theory but bottlenecked by builders with no financial backgrounds shipping basic TradFi features (like fixed-term lending) at a glacial pace of roughly one smart-contract upgrade per year.
insights.speaker: James (Jim)
insights.so_what: The lending opportunity is real but execution risk is the binding constraint; favor teams with serious financial operators over dogmatic decentralization maxis.
insights.quote: Congratulations, we are building something that has been built in TradFi and we're talking about it as an accomplishment

insights.claim: The copied Aave peer-to-pool lending model serves only a niche purpose with aggregate revenue capability around $100M, too small to be a venture-scale opportunity by itself.
insights.speaker: James (Jim)
insights.so_what: Quantifies the ceiling of vanilla DeFi lending; differentiated lending products (off-chain components, new contract sets) are needed to justify venture returns.
insights.quote: the revenue capability of like the aggregate peer-to-pool model being somewhere around like 100 million

insights.claim: Maple Finance is the model for on-chain lending done right because it builds products to serve real demand (including off-chain components) rather than solving self-created decentralization problems.
insights.speaker: James (Jim)
insights.so_what: Pragmatic, demand-driven lending builders that embrace off-chain components are the ones likely to win; Maple is the cited template.
insights.quote: they did not like build a solution to problem that they created

insights.claim: The market is not rewarding tokens for having buyback-and-burn mechanisms; tokens that buy back are down roughly 30-40% median (~55-60% excluding the HYPE outlier), while tokens with no revenue-accrual mechanism are down ~80% over the past year.
insights.speaker: Matt (CMT Digital)
insights.so_what: Buybacks aren't being rewarded, but the absence of revenue mechanisms is being severely punished; screen for revenue-backed tokens to avoid the -80% cohort.
insights.quote: the market isn't really rewarding you for having like a revenue buyback mechanism... the market is punishing you for a lack thereof

insights.claim: Buyback-and-burn is the wrong use of revenue for young projects; even tokens that bought back and burned are still down ~60% (ex-hyperliquid), so spending all revenue on burns is not value-accretive at early maturity.
insights.speaker: host (Boccaccio)
insights.so_what: Challenges the buyback-cult thesis; investors should not over-pay for tokens whose only value story is burning revenue.
insights.quote: if you still bought back then burn your token your token is still down 60%

insights.claim: Token buybacks only signal a project has revenue to deploy, but a token's value is a function of future free cash flows, and most buying-back projects are seeing revenue decline, so it is rational for them to keep falling.
insights.speaker: Noah
insights.so_what: Reframes buybacks as a lagging signal, not a value driver; the forward revenue trajectory is the real determinant, and it's pointing down.
insights.quote: the value of the company is a function of their future free free cash flows

insights.claim: Revenue (REV) and buybacks are irrelevant to token value unless the revenue is actually used to buy back and retire the token or is airdropped directly to holders' wallets.
insights.speaker: Noah
insights.so_what: A hard filter for token selection: demand a direct, enforceable value-accrual path from revenue to holders, or ignore the revenue narrative entirely.
insights.quote: Unless they're using the revenue to buy back the token and it goes away. Or all of the revenue just gets air-dropped to the token holders' wallets... it just does not matter

insights.claim: Buyback-and-burn may only become a meaningful value driver once crypto markets reach greater maturity, analogous to how corporate share buybacks matter more for established companies than year-old startups.
insights.speaker: Aryan (CMT Digital)
insights.so_what: Suggests patience: buyback value-accrual is a late-cycle/mature-market phenomenon, not an early-stage catalyst.
insights.quote: Maybe we just need market maturity for um buyback and burn to actually be valued

insights.claim: ETH and SOL are uninvestable relative to alternatives, with the speaker arguing anyone interested in bidding ETH or SOL 'needs their head reexamined' versus other names in the market.
insights.speaker: James (Jim)
insights.so_what: A strongly bearish relative-value call on the two largest smart-contract L1 tokens; the bar for owning them over alternatives is deemed unmet.
insights.quote: if you being interested in bidding ETH or SOL like you probably need your head reexamined as compared to other stuff in the market

insights.claim: Hyperliquid's tracked treasury wallets have bought more than 2x the cumulative inflows of all the HYPE ETFs since the ETFs went live, and bought over $100M of HYPE in the past week while holding ~$141M in cash (down from ~$160M).
insights.speaker: Ian
insights.so_what: Quantifies the structural HYPE buyer; the cash balance and buying pace are leading indicators for how much support remains under the price.
insights.quote: They've bought like now more than like 2x uh all of the ETFs

insights.claim: HYPE fell from the mid-to-high $60s to ~$58 despite strong structural buying because too many holders are trying to exit simultaneously, with future Coinbase/Circle assistance-fund additions and Grayscale ETF flows not yet contributing meaningfully.
insights.speaker: Ian
insights.so_what: Identifies pending Coinbase/Circle and Grayscale flows as not-yet-priced upside catalysts for HYPE, offset by current exit pressure.

insights.claim: There is froth and peak-euphoria sentiment around HYPE/PERP, including retail and podcast chatter about a GME-style gamma squeeze, which the desk reads as a contrarian warning sign of a likely pullback.
insights.speaker: Ian
insights.so_what: Gamma-squeeze hype is a sentiment top-tell; expect a HYPE pullback even though structural flows remain supportive.
insights.quote: it's like peak euphoria. So, got to keep that in mind as well

insights.claim: Digital-asset treasuries (DATs) face the same reflexive trap as MicroStrategy, where chasing a rising price with more buying mirrors the psychology that left Saylor over-leveraged; even disciplined HYPE buyers risk top-blasting.
insights.speaker: host (Boccaccio)
insights.so_what: Treats DAT buying as behaviorally driven; the risk is they buy tops chasing momentum rather than buying unlocks/dips, a flag for any treasury-backed token.

insights.claim: Hyperliquid reduced its token unlocks by ~90% on chain after realizing continued unlocks would crash the price, a value-protective move most other projects would not make.
insights.speaker: host (Boccaccio)
insights.so_what: Proactive supply management differentiates HYPE's tokenomics from peers; transparent on-chain unlocks plus cuts reduce sell-side overhang risk.
insights.quote: every once in a while hyperliquid posts that they're actually reducing the unlocks by 90%

insights.claim: Hyperliquid is strategically pivoting (via HIP-4) away from revenue generation toward capturing market activity and trade-execution volume, raising questions about whether revenue can keep scaling as it has.
insights.speaker: Aryan (CMT Digital)
insights.so_what: A volume-capture strategy may cap HYPE revenue growth; investors relying on revenue-scaling theses should reassess as the protocol moves to later HIP iterations.
insights.quote: we're seeing hyperliquid move away from revenue generation towards capturing the market activity and trade execution volume

insights.claim: Large players exited around BTC $100K via the ETFs (including a ~$1B block trade of IBIT roughly two weeks ago), illustrating the same FOMO-then-distribution pattern that DATs risk repeating.
insights.speaker: Ian
insights.so_what: Large IBIT block prints around the $100K BTC level mark distribution by big holders; a template for spotting DAT-driven tops.
insights.quote: the largest ever block trade of IBIT is like 2 weeks ago or something... Someone sold like 1 billion of IBIT

insights.claim: Token transparency measures (Blockworks transparency reports, the TTF, team self-reporting of treasury and market-maker wallets) are a major net positive that brings crypto closer to TradFi standards and aids the investing community.
insights.speaker: Matt (CMT Digital)
insights.so_what: Wallet/flow transparency is becoming an edge and a screening tool; favor tokens that self-report and disclose treasury/MM wallet activity.
insights.quote: the added transparency measures that have been implemented... is a massive net benefit to the space

insights.claim: Tracking post-TGE token flows is extremely difficult because minted tokens are distributed across hundreds of addresses, making it hard to distinguish team, investor and community supply without disclosure.
insights.speaker: Matt (CMT Digital)
insights.so_what: Opacity of post-TGE distribution is a structural information gap; disclosure regimes that close it create real informational alpha for analysts.

insights.claim: The best forward token bets are those with real flows tied to them representing some underlying, and tokens NOT appearing in transparency reports (i.e., that resist disclosure) should be avoided.
insights.speaker: Aryan (CMT Digital)
insights.so_what: A selection heuristic: prefer disclosed, flow-backed tokens; treat opacity as a red flag.
insights.quote: any tokens with real real flows tied to them

insights.claim: Maple Finance (Syrup) trades at ~$120-130M (six times sales) as the #2 centralized borrowing player behind Tether, undercutting on fees while making ~$20M revenue, with operating leverage to a sub-20x earnings multiple if its borrow book doubles and a 5-10x outcome if it expands into fintech lending.
insights.speaker: Noah
insights.so_what: A concrete, multiple-anchored long thesis: cheap on sales with credible operating leverage and optionality, and borrow demand that rises in volatile dislocations.
insights.quote: trading at six times sales here and I think they could potentially flex up their take rate if interest rates on chain expand

insights.claim: MetaDAO trades at ~$50-60M (with $10M cash, doing $1-2M revenue) and could become a standard for enforcing that tokens represent company value; scaling its raise cadence to 2-3x could put it below 10x sales with large upside optionality.
insights.speaker: Noah
insights.so_what: A higher-risk, earlier-stage long: differentiated, hard-to-replicate model with growth optionality if it scales fundraising throughput.
insights.quote: right now if they're like 50 million adjusted market cap, they're doing like a million to two of revenue and they can expand the number of launches they're doing by two to three X

insights.claim: BNB is an interesting under-owned long out of left field due to a West-East perception divide, a genuinely good underlying business, and a long track record of routing business success into the token via buyback-and-burn.
insights.speaker: Danny (Blockworks)
insights.so_what: Contrarian large-cap pick: Western dislike and 'HYPE kills BNB' narrative may underprice a profitable business with proven token value-accrual.
insights.quote: I think they've like historically shown that they're willing to shovel some amount of revenues or like success of the business into the token

insights.claim: Derive (on-chain options) is a high-conviction pick that has sold off less than the broader market while hitting all-time-high volumes, on the thesis that options will keep gaining crypto traction even if less than perps.
insights.speaker: Carlos (Blockworks Research)
insights.so_what: Relative-strength plus secular adoption: Derive's resilient volume growth flags on-chain options as an under-served, growing niche.
insights.quote: Hasn't sold off as much as the rest of the market. And they keep just crushing it in terms of traction

insights.claim: There is a real lack of liquid options markets for many crypto assets, leaving investors unable to hedge or express short views via options.
insights.speaker: host (Boccaccio)
insights.so_what: Confirms unmet demand for crypto options infrastructure, supporting the Derive/on-chain-options thesis.
insights.quote: I found out that there's no options for it. And then that's when I realized like, oh, I would like some options in crypto

insights.claim: Multiple desk participants converge on HYPE/hyperliquid as the single most obvious crypto long, with Carlos pairing it with Sky (stablecoins/credit), MetaDAO, and Derive as a four-token book.
insights.speaker: Carlos (Blockworks Research)
insights.so_what: Consensus long among the desk is HYPE plus a small basket of revenue/flow-backed names; useful as a crowded-but-conviction watchlist.

insights.claim: Jito (JTO) is interesting as a now-decoupled-from-Solana bet where large buyers have accumulated single-digit percentages of supply, though the exchange isn't live so it is a bet on potential and vibes.
insights.speaker: Ian
insights.so_what: Speculative accumulation play: large-holder buying and SOL decoupling are the bull signals, offset by unproven product and remaining unlocks.
insights.quote: it's like fully decoupled from Solana at this point

insights.claim: Token markets face an existential risk of ceasing to exist because they have not produced anything of value, making it necessary (though not sufficient) for tokens to actually represent the value of the underlying company.
insights.speaker: Noah
insights.so_what: A sweeping bear-case backdrop: the survival of token markets depends on real value-accrual, raising the bar for every token and favoring value-enforcing models like MetaDAO.
insights.quote: token markets are somewhat at like a existential risk of ceasing to exist because they haven't produced anything of value

insights.claim: Desks are currently selling their entire books, implying a base case of three-to-six months of chop and accumulation before any sustainable bullish setup, with tracked names still ~30-50% above the speaker's fair-value range.
insights.speaker: Noah
insights.so_what: Forced book liquidation argues against an immediate V-bottom; expect extended chop, and note fair values are still well below current prices for tracked names.
insights.quote: my base case is like best best case is you know, we have 3 6 months of of chop and accumulation
